label: Porto Seguro — Cabral Landing (1500)
sub: Site of Pedro Álvares Cabral's landfall on April 22, 1500, marking the beginning of Portuguese colonization of Brazil.

label: Salvador — First Colonial Capital
sub: Founded in 1549 as Brazil's first capital and hub of the Atlantic slave trade. Largest disembarkation port for enslaved Africans in the Americas.

label: Quilombo dos Palmares — Maroon Resistance
sub: Largest maroon community in the Americas (c.1605–1695), sheltering 20,000–30,000 escaped enslaved people. Destroyed by colonial forces in 1695. Led by Zumbi dos Palmares.

label: Ouro Preto — Inconfidência Mineira (1789)
sub: Center of the 1789 Inconfidência Mineira independence conspiracy, inspired by the American and French revolutions. Site of the Museu do Inconfidência.

label: Rio de Janeiro — Former Capital
sub: Colonial and imperial capital (1763–1960). Site of Portuguese royal court's arrival in 1808, independence proclamation ceremonies, and republican proclamation in 1889.

label: São Paulo — Industrial & Financial Capital
sub: Brazil's largest city and economic engine. Center of coffee oligarchy (1889–1930), 1932 Constitutionalist Revolution, ABC metalworkers' strikes (1978–80), and Lula's political rise.

label: Brasília — New Capital (1960)
sub: Built in 41 months and inaugurated April 21, 1960. Designed by Niemeyer and Costa. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Site of January 8, 2023 riots against democracy.

label: Curitiba — Operation Lava Jato HQ
sub: Federal 13th Criminal Court of Curitiba, run by Judge Sérgio Moro, was the epicenter of Operation Car Wash (2014–2021), the largest corruption investigation in Brazilian history.

label: Petrópolis — Imperial Palace
sub: Summer residence and symbol of Dom Pedro II's empire (1843–1889). Museu Imperial houses the imperial crown and princess Isabel's pen used to sign the Lei Áurea abolishing slavery in 1888.

label: Manaus — Amazon Rubber Boom
sub: Center of the Amazon rubber boom (1850s–1912). Its Teatro Amazonas opera house (1896) symbolizes the opulence of the rubber era. Now a Free Trade Zone and gateway to Amazon monitoring.

label: Pará — Amazon Deforestation Frontline
sub: Pará state accounts for the largest share of Brazilian Amazon deforestation. INPE satellite monitoring shows 6,288 km² deforested in 2024 across Legal Amazon — lowest in 9 years under Lula.

label: Porto Alegre — World Social Forum
sub: Host of the inaugural World Social Forum (2001), symbol of Brazil's role in the global alter-globalization movement. Also hosted massive 1984 Diretas Já rallies.

label: Belo Horizonte — June 2013 Protests
sub: June 2013 saw Brazil's largest protests since Diretas Já, triggered by bus fare hikes in São Paulo. Millions marched across cities including Belo Horizonte demanding improved public services and against corruption.

label: Recife — Northeast Economic Hub
sub: Capital of Pernambuco, center of the Brazilian Northeast — historically the poorest region. Hub of Bolsa Família beneficiaries and site of major Dutch colonial presence (1630–1654).

label: Foz do Iguaçu — Itaipu Hydroelectric Dam
sub: Site of the Itaipu binational dam (Brazil-Paraguay), completed in 1984. World's second-largest hydroelectric plant by capacity (14 GW). Supplies ~12% of Brazil's electricity and 76% of Paraguay's.

label: Rio de Janeiro — Complexo do Alemão
sub: Large favela complex in northern Rio, site of major 2010 military/police pacification operation under UPP program. Symbol of Brazil's chronic urban inequality and security crisis.

label: Santo André / ABC Region — Metalworkers' Strikes
sub: The ABC metalworkers' strikes (1978–1980) in São Paulo's industrial suburbs launched Lula's political career and broke the dictatorship-era labor silence. Pivotal to Brazil's redemocratization.

label: Belém — Amazon Hub & COP30 (2025)
sub: Capital of Pará state and gateway to the Amazon. Hosted COP30 climate conference in November 2025. Lula government used Belém hosting to showcase Amazon deforestation reduction achievements.

label: Guarujá — Lula Apartment Controversy
sub: Coastal resort city at center of Operation Lava Jato charges against Lula: a beachfront apartment allegedly given to Lula by contractor OAS. Judge Sérgio Moro convicted Lula in 2017; STF annulled conviction in 2021.

label: Volta Redonda — National Steel Company (CSN)
sub: Site of the Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional (CSN), Brazil's first integrated steelmill, built during Vargas's Estado Novo (1941–1946). Symbol of Brazilian industrialization and nationalist economic development.

label: Canudos, Bahia — War of Canudos (1896–1897)
sub: Site of the War of Canudos, Brazil's bloodiest internal conflict. Antônio Conselheiro's millenarian community of ~25,000 sertanejos defeated four federal army expeditions before being annihilated in October 1897. Immortalized in Euclides da Cunha's 'Os Sertões.'

label: Contestado Region, SC/PR — Contestado War (1912–1916)
sub: A 4-year millenarian peasant uprising in the contested border zone between Santa Catarina and Paraná states. Sparked by land dispossession from a US-owned railway company. Over 20,000 killed. One of Brazil's most forgotten conflicts.

label: Brumadinho, MG — Vale Dam Collapse (Jan 25, 2019)
sub: Vale SA's Córrego do Feijão tailings dam failed on January 25, 2019, killing 270 people. The deadliest mine dam disaster in Brazilian history. Vale reached a R$37.7 billion settlement in 2021. Criminal charges against executives are ongoing.

label: Mariana, MG — Samarco / Fundão Dam (Nov 5, 2015)
sub: Samarco's Fundão tailings dam failed November 5, 2015, releasing 40–60 million m³ of iron ore waste into the Doce River — Brazil's worst environmental disaster. 19 killed, entire river ecosystem destroyed for 650 km to the sea. Remediation costs estimated at R$100 billion.

label: Rio Branco / Xapuri, Acre — Chico Mendes Legacy
sub: Xapuri, Acre — site of rubber tapper activist Chico Mendes' life and murder on December 22, 1988. Mendes pioneered the 'extractive reserve' model protecting Amazon communities. His assassination catalyzed international environmental pressure on Brazil. Acre's capital Rio Branco is his legacy city.

label: Xingu River, Pará — Belo Monte Hydroelectric Dam
sub: Third-largest hydroelectric dam in the world (11.2 GW), completed 2016 on the Xingu River. Displaced ~20,000 people and flooded indigenous and quilombola territories. Subject of decade-long indigenous rights campaign. Justified by Brazil's electricity needs but condemned by environmental groups.

label: Mato Grosso — Amazon Soy Frontier
sub: Brazil's largest soy-producing state and primary driver of Amazon deforestation. The 'arc of deforestation' runs through Mato Grosso, Pará, and Rondônia. INPE data shows Mato Grosso as second-highest deforester after Pará. 'Operation Arc of Fire' (2008) was the first major federal enforcement here.

label: Maracanã Stadium, Rio — 1950 & 2014 World Cups
sub: Largest stadium in South America (capacity 78,838). Site of the 1950 World Cup final 'Maracanazo' where Uruguay defeated Brazil 2-1 before 200,000 fans. Also hosted the 2014 World Cup final (Germany 1-0 Argentina) and 2016 Olympics opening ceremony. Symbol of Brazil's football identity.

label: Pantanal — Catastrophic Fires (2024)
sub: The Pantanal — world's largest tropical wetland — saw its worst fire season in recorded history in 2024, burning 2.78 million hectares (over 17% of Brazilian Pantanal). Combined effects of La Niña drought and climate change. Home to jaguars, giant otters, hyacinth macaws, and 4,700 plant species.

label: Fortaleza, Ceará — Northeast Social Programs Hub
sub: Capital of Ceará state and symbol of the Brazilian Northeast — historically marginalized and among the poorest regions. Among the cities with the highest concentration of Bolsa Família beneficiaries. Ceará is also Brazil's leading state in wind and solar energy investment, a model for green development in the semi-arid Northeast.
